q: What does periodontal staging primarily describe?
explanation: Stage = how bad / how hard to treat.

q: What does periodontal grading primarily describe?
options: Rate of progression and risk profile

q: A patient with many complex factors (vertical defects, furcation, bite collapse) is most consistent with which idea?
Grade C periodontitis implies:
Grade A periodontitis implies:
Clinical attachment level (CAL) is measured from:
explanation: CAL accounts for recession vs PD.

q: Probing depth alone fails to fully describe damage because:
options: PD is always equal to CAL

q: Bleeding on probing primarily indicates:
True periodontal pocket involves:
options: Only swollen gingiva without attachment loss
Apical migration of junctional epithelium / attachment loss

q: Pseudopocket is mainly due to:
explanation: Edema/hyperplasia can deepen PD without CAL loss.

q: Glickman Class I furcation means:
options: Incipient involvement; probe catches but not horizontal into furcation deeply

q: Glickman Class II furcation means:
options: Cul-de-sac; horizontal loss into furcation but not through-and-through

q: Glickman Class III furcation means:
Glickman Class IV furcation means:
options: Through-and-through with gingival recession making furcation visible

q: Most common root for furcation issues on mandibular molars relates to:
options: Bifurcation anatomy and bone loss pattern
explanation: Mandibular molars have buccal/lingual furcation access.

q: Primary etiologic agent of plaque-induced gingivitis is:
explanation: Biofilm is necessary cause for plaque-induced disease.

q: Smoking effect on periodontium includes:
options: Worse disease, impaired healing, may mask bleeding

q: Diabetes and periodontitis relationship is best described as:
options: Perio never affects glycemic control considerations

q: Drug-induced gingival enlargement classic triad includes:
Best first management step for drug-induced gingival enlargement often includes:
options: Immediate full mouth extraction only option
Oral hygiene + plaque control ± drug substitution coordination

q: Foundation of cause-related non-surgical perio therapy is:
options: Scaling and root planing (SRP) with OH instruction
Only free gingival graft first always

q: Re-evaluation after SRP is typically used to:
options: Assess residual pockets/inflammation and plan further care

q: Systemic antibiotics as perio therapy are best thought as:
options: Adjuncts in selected cases, not sole therapy

q: Indications for periodontal surgery often include:
options: Persistent deep pockets / access for root debridement / regenerative opportunities after cause control
explanation: Surgery after or with cause control as indicated.

q: Guided tissue regeneration aims to:
options: Regenerate periodontal attachment apparatus under barrier principles

q: Biological width (supracrestal tissue attachment) violation often leads to:
Approximate classic teaching height of biologic width / supracrestal attachment is about:
options: ~2 mm combined connective + epithelial attachment (classic exam figure)

q: Peri-implant mucositis is characterized by:
options: Soft tissue inflammation without progressive crestal bone loss of peri-implantitis

q: Peri-implantitis includes:
options: Inflammation with progressive bone loss around implant

q: Primary prevention of peri-implant disease emphasizes:
Necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis classic features include:
options: Painless white plaque only that never bleeds
Pain, bleeding, necrosis of papillae, often fetor; stress/smoking risk
explanation: NUG is acute painful necrosis pattern.

q: Periodontal abscess management cornerstone is:
options: Antibiotics alone without local care always enough
Drainage + debridement of cause + risk control
Immediate RCT of every tooth always

q: Miller mobility Class II roughly means:
options: Increased mobility in horizontal direction beyond normal, not depressible (classic teaching bands)

q: Primary occlusal trauma means:
options: Injury from excessive occlusal force on a periodontium of normal support

q: Secondary occlusal trauma means:
options: Normal or excess forces on a reduced periodontium

q: Old term 'aggressive periodontitis' maps roughly to:
options: Rapidly progressive / Grade C-type patterns in modern thinking

q: Most important daily patient factor for perio success is:
options: Monthly professional care only without home care

q: When residual deep pockets remain after excellent SRP and OH, next often considered is:
options: Surgical access / further periodontal therapy as indicated

q: Radiographic bone loss assessment in staging is used as:
options: Part of severity/complexity appraisal (with clinical findings)

q: Furcation involvement generally increases:
Horizontal bone loss pattern is often associated with:
options: Suprabony pockets / relatively even loss patterns

q: Vertical/angular bone defects may be candidates for:
Probe force should be:
Gingivitis without attachment loss is:
Maintenance interval after active therapy is often:
Local drug delivery (e.g., antimicrobials in pocket) is:
Pregnancy-associated gingival changes are mainly related to:
HIV-related perio presentations historically include:
Tooth mobility may improve after:
options: Inflammation control and/or occlusal adjustment when indicated

q: Prognosis worsens with:
options: Only young age always good alone
Uncontrolled systemic disease, smoking, poor OH, advanced furcation

q: Which drug is classically linked to gingival enlargement (item 1)?
explanation: nifedipine is in the classic triad.

q: Which drug is classically linked to gingival enlargement (item 2)?
explanation: phenytoin is in the classic triad.

q: Which drug is classically linked to gingival enlargement (item 3)?
explanation: cyclosporine is in the classic triad.

q: In 2017 classification, Stage I primarily indicates:
explanation: Stage I is a severity/complexity band.

q: In 2017 classification, Stage II primarily indicates:
explanation: Stage II is a severity/complexity band.

q: In 2017 classification, Stage III primarily indicates:
options: Severe with potential for tooth loss complexity
explanation: Stage III is a severity/complexity band.

q: In 2017 classification, Stage IV primarily indicates:
explanation: Stage IV is a severity/complexity band.

q: Deep residual 7 mm pockets after good SRP, good OH, BOP residual — next logical step?
options: Consider surgical access / further perio therapy planning

q: Smoker with pale gingiva and little BOP but radiographic bone loss — concern?
options: Smoking can mask bleeding despite disease

q: Uncontrolled diabetes, progressive perio — management priority includes:
options: Medical control coordination + periodontal cause-related care
Only extract all immediately without medical liaison

q: Crown margin deep subgingival with chronic inflammation and bone loss adjacent — think:
options: Possible biologic width violation / plaque trap

q: Implant with bleeding, swelling, no progressive bone loss on serial films — most like:
options: Vertical root fracture of natural tooth only

q: Implant with progressive crater bone loss and inflammation — most like:
Grade B vs C decision often weighs:
options: Evidence of progression rate and risk factors (e.g., smoking, diabetes)

q: Best way to monitor home care success clinically includes:
options: Plaque scores, BOP reduction, patient demonstration

q: Furcation Class III on a strategic molar may require:
options: Complex management options (root resection, regenerative attempt, tunnel, or extraction/plan)

q: Occlusal adjustment in perio is:
options: Adjunctive when traumatic occlusion contributes — not a substitute for plaque control

q: High-yield perio principle #68: plaque biofilm role is best stated as:
options: Necessary primary etiology for plaque-induced gingival/periodontal disease
Irrelevant if patient flosses once a year
